RULING - A R G U E D. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

OTHER COE 1's Motion For Protective Order.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Motion for Protective Order Granted in Part and Denied in Part. The motion is granted to the extent discovery seeks the actual report or reports made by the responding party as mandated by Cal. Penal Code 11166, et seq. The motion is denied to the extent discovery seeks responsive information to discovery regardless of whether it may be contained in any of said reports. The fact that discoverable information in the possession of a responding party may also be contained in a report made pursuant to Cal. Penal Code 11166, et seq. does not thereafter magically transform what was once discoverable into something that may never be seen or heard of again.

The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312 directly by email to the Judge Pro Tem at jyjudgeprotem@gmail.com.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-09-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Daniel C. Zamora of the law offices of Weintraub Tobin (415-772-9639) appears as counsel for defendants/demurring parties, Does 1 and 3

Attorney, Alison M. Crane of the law offices of Bledsoe Diestel Treppa & Crane LLP (415-981-5411) appears as counsel for defendant/demurring party, Doe 4

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is submitted without argument. The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANTS USA WEST PROVINCE, SOCIETY OF JESUS, AND ST. IGNATIUS CHURCH'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT, as to cause of action one for public nuisance is SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. Plaintiff's request for damages on this claim is duplicative of the negligence claim. "Where negligence and nuisance causes of action rely on the same facts about lack of due care, the nuisance claim is a negligence claim." (El Escorial Owners' Ass'n v. DLC Plastering, Inc. (2007) 154 Cal.App.4th 1337, 1349.) Plaintiff also lacks standing for prospective relief. Section 3493 of the Civil Code specifically provides that "A private person may maintain an action for a public nuisance, if it is specially injurious to himself, but not otherwise." (Italics added.) The face of the complaint demonstrates that plaintiff is now an adult and similarly situated to other members of the public. Therefore, he lacks standing to maintain the claim. (See Venuto v. Owens-Corning Fiberglass Corp. (1971) 22 Cal.App.3d 116, 123-125.) Lastly, the Legislature has already made a policy choice regarding the reporting of sex abuse under Penal Code sec. 11164 et seq. and adding court-made disclosure requirements would cause confusion.

The demurrer to cause of action seven for breach of fiduciary duty is SUSTAINED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. Though paragraphs 64-65 of the complaint refer to plaintiff's vulnerability based on his age and loss of his father, the crux of the claim is that plaintiff was vulnerable because of the priests' status and therefore, the claim fails. (See Richelle L. v. Roman Catholic Archbishop of San Francisco (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 257, 281-282.)

The demurrers to causes of action five, six, and ten are SUSTAINED WITH LEAVE TO AMEND. Plaintiff has leave to amend to plead a cause of action for active concealment. Generally, a duty to disclose only arises where a fiduciary/confidential relationship exists. However, absent such a relationship, a duty to disclose also arises where: (1) the defendant has exclusive knowledge; (2) the defendant has actively concealed the truth; or (3) the defendant partially suppresses the truth. (See Heliotis v. Schuman (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 646, 651.) Plaintiff also has leave to amend to plead a cause of action for intentional misrepresentation with more particularity.

Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: not reported. (L&M302)

Generally, a duty to disclose only arises where a fiduciary/confidential relationship exists. However, absent such a relationship, a duty to disclose also arises where: (1) the defendant has exclusive knowledge; (2) the defendant has actively concealed the truth; or (3) the defendant partially suppresses the truth. (See Heliotis v. Schuman (1986) 181 Cal.App.3d 646, 651.) Plaintiff also has leave to amend to plead a cause of action for intentional misrepresentation with more particularity.

Written order is signed and filed in open court.
